change_summary: C3 patches applied (ISS-1815 major / 1816 moderate / 1817 moderate). Verifying that detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text now (a) cite Part 5 instead of stale 'Kill-Shot #1', (b) include nested-model triviality, (c) preempt WGS84-circularity. detail_evidence grew 1624→2321c (+697c, +43%); detail_verdict_text grew 973→991c (+18c, +1.8%). Verdict 'Misleading' unchanged.
our_argument_summary: Misleading verdict on the elliptical disc geometry claim, now covering five distinct lines of attack: (1) AIC/BIC required for nested-model selection — adding free parameters always reduces residuals; (2) NEW: ellipse-vs-circle is mathematically a nested-model test where b/a=1 collapses ellipse to circle, so 'ellipse never lost across V5–V9' is structurally guaranteed not empirically informative; (3) the 48.6% RMS reduction is unverifiable (no script in repo computes it); (4) inter-city distances are WGS84-derived, so the ellipse absorbs azimuthal-equidistant projection distortion rather than measuring disc shape — and NEW: those distances are independently confirmed by radar ranging, fiber-optic latency, VLBI baseline interferometry; (5) b/a wanders 0.70–0.90 (29% drift) across V5–V9 vs WGS84 b/a=0.997 from first principles; (6) dome's own OPEN-003 admits ellipse parameters still converging.

relevant_files: generate_advanced_map_v2.py (hardcoded a=20015, b=15000, orient=60° per c3)
test_curve_stretching.py (ellipse test uses sqrt(1-(r/R)²) — semicircle, not true ellipse)
find_curve.py (8 cities, WGS84 distances, no ellipse comparison)
raw-text/08-live-power.txt (dome's own GPS Sagnac Verification + OpenSky satellite tracking + Falsification: 'Model error >0.1% vs WGS84 standard' — confirms dome itself uses WGS84 as benchmark)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Unchanged from c3. No pipeline computes the 48.6% RMS reduction. Visualization scripts hardcode ellipse parameters; find_curve.py has no elliptical correction; no circular-vs-elliptical comparison exists. The c3 patches modify only review prose, not dome-side code claims, so the c2/c3 dome_code_analysis remains accurate.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Unchanged from c3. All ellipse parameters hardcoded (a=20015, b=15000, orient=60°). The b/a 0.70–0.90 range is asserted in descriptive text and confirmed by raw-text/05-model.txt. The 48.6% RMS figure exists only in prose; no script in raw-text/ produces it.
data_sources_used: WGS84 reference distances between 8 city pairs (hardcoded in find_curve.py per c2/c3)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Unchanged from c3. OPEN-003 ('ellipse parameters still converging') and OPEN-001 ('dome-native coordinates without WGS84 — IN PROGRESS' per raw-text/05-model.txt and raw-text/07-ai-context.txt) are open-ended retreat positions. The dome's own falsification spec (raw-text/08-live-power.txt: 'Model error >0.1% vs WGS84 standard') uses WGS84 as the benchmark — this is the clearest internal evidence that the dome cannot consistently dispute WGS84-derived distances while measuring its own success against them.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. The post-c3-patch detail_evidence accurately characterizes the visualization-only role of the ellipse parameters and the absence of an RMS computation script. The new multi-method-metrology sentence is consistent with the dome's own explicit reliance on WGS84 as falsification standard.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Per c3: monitor.py contains no domain or fetch logic for elliptical-disc geometry; the 8-city distance evaluation in find_curve.py runs script-on-invocation only. raw-text/02-wins.txt re-confirms the WIN is described as static '48.6% RMS reduction, never reversed' rather than a fetched-and-compared metric. No change to this tag.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Per c3: standard cartography (Snyder, USGS Professional Paper 1395) explains the latitude-dependent radial distance distortion of the polar azimuthal equidistant projection of a sphere. The dome relabels this distortion as 'the disc is elliptical' while continuing to fit globe-derived inter-city distances. raw-text/06-evolution.txt's own statement ('This was correct for circular disc + WGS84 coordinates') reveals that the model was always operating in WGS84 space.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Per c3: all inter-city distances were established by 20th-century geodesy decades before the dome model fit them. raw-text/05-model.txt's b/a 0.70-0.90 V5-V9 trajectory confirms the parameter is being fit version-by-version rather than predicted in advance.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Per c3: a, b, orient are empirical curve-fit outputs against WGS84 distances. The c3 patch does not alter this — the new sentences strengthen the 'fit not derivation' argument by adding the multi-method confirmation that WGS84 inter-city distances are not themselves a globe-presupposition.

holes_found.description: GPS-AS-INDEPENDENT-METHOD WEAK LINK in the new WGS84-circularity preemption (ISS-1817 patch). The patch lists four independent confirmation methods: 'radar ranging, GPS time-of-flight, fiber-optic latency, and VLBI baseline interferometry — methods that are not geometrically committed to a globe shape yet converge on the same inter-city distances as WGS84 computation.' Three of the four are strong (radar ranging measures Euclidean signal propagation; fiber-optic latency measures cable-path length, which is a lower bound on inter-city geodesic distance; VLBI uses extragalactic radio sources to derive baselines independently of any Earth-shape model). GPS, however, is the weakest example: although signal-propagation timings are frame-independent, the standard GPS position-fix solver internally uses the WGS84 ellipsoid as its reference. A pedantic dome defender could attack: 'GPS positions ARE WGS84 outputs — citing GPS as independent confirmation of WGS84 is circular.' The defender's counter is technically partial — the dome itself uses GPS Sagnac verification (raw-text/08-live-power.txt) and explicitly benchmarks against WGS84, so the dome cannot consistently dispute GPS-derived distances. But the rhetorical exposure is real: a careful reader sees 'GPS time-of-flight' and registers it as the weakest of four cited methods. Lower priority than the c3 patch achievement; this is sharpening, not a substantive hole.
holes_found.recommendation: Either drop 'GPS time-of-flight' (leaving radar ranging, fiber-optic latency, VLBI — three strong methods) OR replace it with 'satellite ranging via independent ground-station baselines' which is geometrically more honest. Alternative: keep the GPS reference but add a one-clause acknowledgment '(GPS uses WGS84 internally for position fixes, but the underlying signal-propagation timings between ground stations are frame-independent and would differ on a flat-disc geometry)' — most rigorous but adds length. Lowest-effort fix: simply remove 'GPS time-of-flight' from the comma list.

holes_found.description: NESTED-MODEL SENTENCE COULD CITE THE FORMAL PRINCIPLE. The new sentence 'setting b/a = 1 collapses the ellipse to a circle, so an ellipse fit can never produce a worse RMS than a circle fit on the same data' is correct but states the principle without anchoring it to the standard literature. A reader sympathetic to the dome could read this as 'the critic just asserted nested models trivially win — show me a textbook reference.' The c3 stronger_arguments already cited Burnham & Anderson (2002) Sections 1.2-2.5 on nested models; the patch did not link that citation into the new sentence. Severity is minor because the sentence is mathematically self-evident to anyone who has done curve fitting; but in a public review aimed at non-specialist readers, an inline anchor (DOI 10.1007/978-0-387-98135-2 already cited in the same paragraph for AIC/BIC) would be reassuring.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: amend the nested-model sentence to read '...so an ellipse fit can never produce a worse RMS than a circle fit on the same data (Burnham & Anderson, Model Selection and Multimodel Inference, §1.2–§2.5).' This re-uses the existing AIC/BIC citation which already lives one sentence earlier — zero new citations needed. Low priority; do not block on this.

holes_found.description:
DETAIL_EVIDENCE GROWING LONG (2321c, +43% from c3 baseline 1624c). After the c3 patches the first paragraph (before the

) is now ~1700c — at the upper end of comfortable reading for a single block. The TLDR_evidence (357c) still summarizes the AIC/BIC critique only and does not cover the WGS84-circularity preemption or the nested-model triviality, so a reader who clicks expand sees substantially more material than the TLDR signaled. This is not a TLDR factual error (the TLDR still accurately summarizes the principal critique), but it is a TLDR coverage gap relative to the expanded text. Minor severity per the curmudgeon's TLDR-imprecision-not-misleading rule.
holes_found.recommendation:
Optional: split the first paragraph at the WGS84-circularity boundary by inserting a

after '...mathematically guaranteed by the model structure, not by any physical signal.' This separates the methodological critique (AIC/BIC + nested-model) from the projection-distortion critique (WGS84 + multi-method confirmation), improving scannability without changing content. Lower priority: optional TLDR refresh to mention 'and WGS84-derived inter-city distances explain the residual structure as projection distortion, not a real disc shape' — but keeping the TLDR short is also defensible.

holes_found.description: ISS-1818 (post-hoc boilerplate could specifically note 'derivation is itself a curve fit') was correctly classified minor by the decider and left open. No re-litigation here — the boilerplate is still functional. Flagging only to confirm c3-->c4 traceability: 4 c3 holes, 3 patched, 1 deferred, no regressions.
holes_found.recommendation: No action required; ISS-1818 remains in open-issues.json as documented intent. Future curmudgeon runs can sharpen if and when WIN-030 is otherwise being edited.

stronger_arguments.description: DOME'S OWN WGS84 BENCHMARK — sharper version of the c3 GPS-metrology argument. raw-text/08-live-power.txt explicitly states the dome's falsification criterion: 'Model error >0.1% vs WGS84 standard'. The dome model has chosen WGS84 as its own success metric. So the WGS84-circularity defense the c3 review preempted ('you've assumed WGS84 in order to debunk us') is even more decisively self-defeating: the dome cannot use WGS84 as its falsification benchmark while disputing WGS84-derived city-pair distances. This is a stronger (one-sentence) preemption than the multi-method-metrology preemption that ISS-1817 already added. Optional future patch: add a single sentence like 'The dome model itself benchmarks its own predictions against WGS84 (raw-text/08-live-power.txt: "Falsification: Model error >0.1% vs WGS84 standard"), so disputing WGS84-derived inter-city distances would also invalidate the dome's own self-evaluation.'
sources: raw-text/08-live-power.txt — dome's GPS Sagnac Verification domain and 'Model error >0.1% vs WGS84 standard' falsification spec
raw-text/05-model.txt — OPEN-001 'Dome-native coordinates without WGS84 (IN PROGRESS)' — dome admits it does not have non-WGS84 coordinates

stronger_arguments.description: OPEN-001 / OPEN-003 / OPEN-006 PATTERN — the dome maintains at least three open items (per raw-text/05-model.txt and raw-text/07-ai-context.txt) acknowledging that key parameters and coordinate systems are unfinished: dome-native coordinates without WGS84 (OPEN-001), ellipse parameters still converging (OPEN-003), WGS84 latitude bias at high latitudes (OPEN-006). This is a coordinated pattern: the model defers its hardest commitments by labeling them 'IN PROGRESS' or 'OPEN' and then claims confirmation on derivative results. WIN-030 specifically depends on OPEN-001 (the WGS84 dependency that should be eliminated but hasn't been) and OPEN-003 (the parameters that should converge but haven't). A single sentence mapping the WIN to these open items would expose the structural escape pattern. Low-priority improvement; current text already cites OPEN-003 once.
sources: raw-text/05-model.txt — list of OPEN-001 through OPEN-006
raw-text/07-ai-context.txt — duplicates of OPEN-001 and OPEN-006

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model has correctly identified that distances measured on the surface of a sphere, when forced onto a flat azimuthal-equidistant projection, exhibit systematic latitude-dependent error — and that the residual structure has a roughly elliptical shape (data/wins.json:030 detail_evidence). The c3 patch (ISS-1816) makes this kernel even cleaner by formalizing it as a nested-model relationship: the dome has correctly observed that a 2-parameter family of disc shapes fits globe-derived distances with smaller residual than a 1-parameter family — that is genuinely true, and is in fact the same observation any cartographer would make when trying to flatten a sphere. The dome's own raw-text/06-evolution.txt acknowledgment ('This was correct for circular disc + WGS84 coordinates') shows the model is internally aware that the 'ellipse vs circle' comparison runs in WGS84 space.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Detecting projection distortion on globe-derived data is exactly what spherical geometry predicts (data/wins.json:030 detail_evidence — 'absorbing the radial scaling distortion that the Azimuthal Equidistant projection introduces when a sphere is projected onto a flat disc'). The c3 patch (ISS-1816) makes the structural-triviality argument explicit: an ellipse with free b/a contains the circle as a special case (b/a=1), so an ellipse fit cannot lose to a circle fit on the same data — 'ellipse never lost across V5–V9' is mathematically forced. The c3 patch (ISS-1817) closes the WGS84-circularity escape: inter-city distances are confirmed by radar ranging, fiber-optic latency, and VLBI baseline interferometry — methods that don't presuppose globe shape — and the dome itself benchmarks its own model against WGS84 (raw-text/08-live-power.txt). And the c3-retained b/a wandering point (29% drift across V5–V9 vs WGS84 b/a=0.997) is the empirical signature of fitting rather than measurement. Three independent angles — methodology, geometry, version-stability — converge on the same conclusion.

advocate_mode.best_defense: After c3 patches, the dome defender's strongest remaining attack is the GPS-internal-WGS84 prong (see hole #1 above): 'You list four independent metrology methods to escape the circularity charge, but GPS — the most widely-known of the four — uses WGS84 internally for its position-fix solver. Citing GPS as independent confirmation of WGS84 is itself circular.' This is technically partial (signal-propagation timings ARE frame-independent), but rhetorically it lets a defender focus on the weakest of the four cited methods. Secondary defense: the new nested-model sentence asserts the trivialty without an inline citation, leaving room for 'show me the textbook' deflection — though this is a minor rhetorical exposure since the principle is self-evident to anyone with curve-fitting experience. Tertiary: the dome can still appeal to OPEN-003 ('parameters still converging') as 'we are doing iterative scientific refinement, not curve-fitting' — but this defense was already addressed in c3 by noting WGS84 b/a converges from first principles to 0.997, while the dome's b/a wanders 0.70–0.90 with no anchor.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: The GPS-internal-WGS84 attack is real but minor (hole #1 above) — easiest fix is dropping 'GPS time-of-flight' from the four-method comma list, leaving radar ranging, fiber-optic latency, and VLBI — three methods none of which use WGS84 internally. This is a single-word delete in the source text. Even without that fix, the dome's own use of WGS84 as falsification benchmark (raw-text/08-live-power.txt) means the dome cannot coherently mount the circularity charge — but a reader may not connect those dots. The c3 patches successfully neutralized the major attacks (stale cross-reference, missing nested-model argument, missing WGS84-circularity preemption), and what remains is one minor rhetorical sharpening, not a load-bearing weakness.

cross_win_consistency.notes: C3-c4 unchanged — the cross-WIN compatibility analysis from c3 holds. WIN-027 (Southern distance quadratic, Misleading) makes the same projection-distortion argument with a different dataset. WIN-058 (Unified angular coordinate, Misleading) cites the same OPEN-001 (WGS-84 dependency). WIN-031 (North Pole cosmic mountain, Unfalsifiable) shares the cartographic-fit pattern. The c3 patches strengthened WIN-030's WGS84-circularity preemption but did not introduce any new inter-WIN claims that would risk consistency drift. No defender could compile a 'your review contradicts itself' attack across these four WINs — they are mutually reinforcing on the projection-fit-vs-physical-discovery axis.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 4 review of WIN-030, triggered by priority queue item queue_id=296 covering decider's self-application of three c3-recommended patches (ISS-1815 major, ISS-1816 moderate, ISS-1817 moderate). The decider also created ISS-1818 (minor, post-hoc boilerplate sharpening) and correctly left it open as low priority. Verdict: all three patches close their respective c3 holes cleanly and introduce no regressions. ISS-1815 (stale 'Kill-Shot #1' cross-reference, major): Verified the new text 'see the Kill-Shot section (Part 5) and Part 2b' resolves to existing anchors. Part 5 exists (id='part5', title='Part 5: Kill-Shot Binary Tests') with one named subsection ('ks-april-2026-update'). Part 2b exists (id='part2b', title='Part 2b: Repository Code Analysis'). Both targets are stable. The previously-broken numeric reference is gone. ISS-1816 (nested-model triviality, moderate): The new sentence is mathematically correct — for nested models with free parameters, the lower-dimensional model is a constrained-optimum special case of the higher-dimensional model, so the higher-dimensional fit cannot have larger sum-of-squares. The sentence sits in the right place in the argument flow (immediately after the AIC/BIC penalization claim, before the WGS84-projection critique). It uses accurate technical language ('nested-model test', 'collapses the ellipse to a circle'). One minor rhetorical sharpening available: the principle could be inline-anchored to the AIC/BIC citation already in the paragraph, but this is optional. ISS-1817 (WGS84-circularity preemption, moderate): The new sentence is the load-bearing improvement. It explicitly addresses the strongest dome-defender attack ('you've presupposed WGS84 to debunk us') by citing four metrology methods that don't presuppose globe shape. Three of the four (radar ranging, fiber-optic latency, VLBI baseline interferometry) are strongly independent of WGS84. GPS time-of-flight, the fourth, is the weakest example because GPS position-fix solvers internally use WGS84 — a careful defender could spotlight this as the weak link. The rhetorical exposure is minor and the dome itself uses WGS84 as falsification benchmark (raw-text/08-live-power.txt: 'Model error >0.1% vs WGS84 standard'), which means the dome cannot coherently mount the circularity charge. But the option to drop GPS from the comma list (one-word delete) would tighten the patch. ISS-1818 (post-hoc boilerplate sharpening, minor, deferred): Correctly left open. The boilerplate is functional; sharpening 'derivation is itself a curve fit' is a stylistic improvement, not a substantive hole. No re-litigation here. TLDR coverage check: tldr_evidence (357c) still covers the AIC/BIC critique correctly. After patches, the expanded detail_evidence now includes additional content (nested-model triviality, multi-method WGS84 confirmation) not summarized in the TLDR. This is not a TLDR factual error — the TLDR remains accurate within its scope — but the gap between TLDR scope and expanded-content scope has widened. Minor. Length: detail_evidence is now 2321c. Approaching the upper end of comfortable single-block reading. A

insertion at the WGS84 boundary would improve scannability with zero content change. Minor sharpening, optional. The new findings (one minor on the GPS reference being the weakest of four cited methods, one minor on missing inline citation for the nested-model principle, one minor on TLDR/expanded coverage gap) are all surface polish, not substantive holes. The verdict 'Misleading' continues to hold with confidence raised slightly (0.88 → 0.91) reflecting the closure of the c3 vulnerabilities. Lens A (advocate mode): the dome defender's best remaining attack is the GPS-internal-WGS84 prong (defense_survives=1, down from 3 in c3). The defender lost the WGS84-circularity prong to ISS-1817 and the nested-model-triviality flank to ISS-1816. The remaining minor exposure is sharp-readable but not load-bearing. Lens B (cross-WIN consistency): no change. WIN-030 remains compatible with WIN-027, WIN-058, WIN-031. The patches did not introduce any new inter-WIN claim that risks consistency drift. Lens C (quantitative verification): all three patch texts verified verbatim in current wins.json. Length deltas match expectation. Section anchors all resolve. The dome's own raw-text confirms our characterization of b/a 0.70-0.90 and reveals the dome explicitly benchmarks against WGS84 (a stronger argument we could optionally surface in a future patch — see stronger_arguments above). Finally, considering the other side: a sympathetic reader who concedes the methodological critique might still ask 'is the dome correct that a flat-disc geometry could in principle exhibit elliptical asymmetry?' The answer is yes in principle, but the dome's evidence (R² improvement on globe-derived distances) cannot distinguish between (a) the disc actually being elliptical and (b) the data being globe-derived projection-distortion artifacts. Without an independent measurement of the asymmetry — e.g., direct surveying not anchored to WGS84 — the claim is structurally unfalsifiable in the form presented. The c3 patches strengthen our review's articulation of this distinction without overreaching into 'no flat-disc geometry could ever be elliptical' (which would be a strawman of the dome model). The c3 patches successfully closed the substantive holes. Recommended action: no_change. Optional polish (drop 'GPS time-of-flight', insert

, refresh TLDR, citation-link nested-model sentence) can be deferred to a future low-priority touch-up cycle.
